subtopics.title: Fundamental and Derived Quantities
common_misconceptions: Confusing mass and weight
Using CGS units instead of SI
Not understanding dimensional analysis
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: None. Fundamental quantities have base SI units: length (m), mass (kg), time (s), temperature (K), current (A). Derived quantities: area (m²), volume (m³), velocity (m/s), acceleration (m/s²), force (N = kg·m/s²).

subtopics.title: Measuring Instruments
common_misconceptions: Confusing precision with accuracy
Not accounting for zero error
Reading vernier scale incorrectly
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Vernier reading = main scale reading + (vernier coincidence × least count). Micrometer reading = main scale + (circular scale × 0.01mm). Always check for zero error and apply correction.

subtopics.title: Scalar and Vector Quantities
common_misconceptions: Thinking all quantities with magnitude and direction are vectors
Adding vectors algebraically like scalars
Confusing resultant with equilibrant
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: For perpendicular vectors: R = √(A² + B²), tan θ = B/A. Parallelogram law: R² = P² + Q² + 2PQcosθ. Resolution: Fx = Fcosθ, Fy = Fsinθ.

subtopics.title: Concepts of Motion
common_misconceptions: Confusing distance with displacement
Thinking constant speed means zero acceleration on a curved path
Confusing speed with velocity
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: v = u + at; s = ut + ½at²; v² = u² + 2as; s = ½(u+v)t. For relative velocity: VAB = VA - VB. These equations apply ONLY for constant acceleration.

subtopics.title: Motion Graphs
common_misconceptions: Confusing displacement-time with velocity-time graphs
Not understanding that gradient of s-t graph = velocity
Not understanding that area under v-t graph = displacement
subtopics.teaching_note: Key relationships: Gradient of s-t graph = velocity. Gradient of v-t graph = acceleration. Area under v-t graph = displacement. For uniform acceleration from rest: s = ½at² (parabolic s-t graph).

subtopics.title: Projectile Motion
common_misconceptions: Thinking horizontal motion affects vertical motion
Not resolving initial velocity into components
Using wrong formula for maximum height
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Horizontal: x = ucosθ·t, ax = 0. Vertical: y = usinθ·t - ½gt², vy = usinθ - gt. Max height: H = u²sin²θ/(2g). Range: R = u²sin(2θ)/g. Time of flight: T = 2usinθ/g.

subtopics.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
common_misconceptions: Thinking force is needed to maintain motion
Confusing mass and weight
Not understanding action-reaction pairs act on different bodies
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: F = ma (2nd law). Momentum p = mv. Impulse = Ft = Δ(mv). Weight W = mg. Friction: F = μR. Newton's 3rd: FAB = -FBA (equal and opposite, on DIFFERENT bodies).

subtopics.title: Circular Motion
common_misconceptions: Thinking centrifugal force is a real force
Confusing angular and linear velocity
Not understanding that centripetal force is provided by tension, friction, or gravity
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: v = rω = 2πr/T = 2πrf. Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r = rω². Centripetal force: F = mv²/r = mrω². No centrifugal force - it's a fictitious force in rotating frames.

subtopics.title: Work
common_misconceptions: Work = force × distance (should be displacement in direction of force)
Thinking holding a heavy object does work
Not using cosθ for angled forces
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equation: W = Fs cosθ = F·s (dot product). When force ⊥ displacement, W = 0. Work done by gravity = mgh. Work-energy theorem: W = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mu². Unit: joule (J).

subtopics.title: Energy
common_misconceptions: Energy is lost in transformations - NO, converted!
Confusing energy with force
Thinking PE only exists at height
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: KE = ½mv². GPE = mgh. Conservation: ME = KE + PE = constant (without friction). For a falling body: mgh = ½mv², so v = √(2gh). Total energy in a closed system is conserved.

subtopics.title: Power
common_misconceptions: Confusing power with energy
Using wrong units (watt-hour vs watt)
Thinking 100% efficiency is possible
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: P = W/t = Fv. Efficiency = (useful output / total input) × 100% = (Pout/Pin) × 100%. 1 hp = 746 W. 1 kW = 1000 W. Power is rate, energy is total - like speed vs distance.

subtopics.title: Temperature and Thermometers
common_misconceptions: Confusing heat with temperature
Thinking heat is a substance (caloric theory)
Not converting Celsius to Kelvin for gas laws
subtopics.teaching_note: Key: Temperature is measure of hotness (°C or K). Heat is energy transfer (J). T(K) = T(°C) + 273. Absolute zero = 0 K = -273°C. At absolute zero, molecular motion stops.

subtopics.title: Thermal Expansion
common_misconceptions: All substances expand when heated - water is anomalous below 4°C
Confusing real and apparent expansion
Not understanding bimetallic strip applications
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: ΔL = αL₀Δθ. ΔA = βA₀Δθ = 2αA₀Δθ. ΔV = γV₀Δθ = 3αV₀Δθ. Real expansion = apparent + container expansion. Water contracts from 0°C to 4°C, then expands above 4°C.

subtopics.title: Heat Capacity and Latent Heat
common_misconceptions: Heat capacity and specific heat capacity are the same thing
Temperature rises during phase change
Confusing latent heat of fusion with vaporization
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Q = mcΔθ (sensible heat). Q = mL (latent heat, no temperature change). Heat capacity C = mc. Method of mixtures: heat lost by hot = heat gained by cold. For water: c = 4200 J/kg·K, Lf = 3.34×10⁵ J/kg, Lv = 2.26×10⁶ J/kg.

subtopics.title: Gas Laws
common_misconceptions: Using Celsius instead of Kelvin in gas law calculations
Confusing Boyle's and Charles's law conditions
Not understanding that ideal gases don't exist
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Boyle's law: P ∝ 1/V (constant T) → P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. Charles's law: V ∝ T (constant P) → V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. Pressure law: P ∝ T (constant V). Combined: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂. Ideal gas: PV = nRT where R = 8.31 J/mol·K.

subtopics.title: Properties of Waves
common_misconceptions: Confusing transverse and longitudinal waves
Thinking amplitude affects wave speed
Not understanding the relationship between frequency and period
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: v = fλ. T = 1/f. For transverse waves: displacement ⊥ direction of travel (light, water). For longitudinal: displacement ∥ direction (sound). Wave speed depends on medium, NOT on f or λ.

subtopics.title: Wave Phenomena
common_misconceptions: Confusing diffraction with refraction
Thinking all waves need a medium
Not understanding constructive vs destructive interference
subtopics.teaching_note: Key principles: Reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Refraction: wave changes speed and direction at medium boundary. Diffraction: spreading through gaps. Interference: constructive (in phase, crest+crest) vs destructive (out of phase, crest+trough).

subtopics.title: Propagation of Light
common_misconceptions: Light travels instantaneously
Shadows are the same size as objects
Not understanding umbra vs penumbra
subtopics.teaching_note: Key: Speed of light in vacuum c = 3×10⁸ m/s. Light travels slower in other media. Umbra = total shadow (total eclipse). Penumbra = partial shadow (partial eclipse). Solar eclipse: Moon between Sun and Earth. Lunar eclipse: Earth between Sun and Moon.

subtopics.title: Reflection and Mirrors
common_misconceptions: Virtual images can only be seen in plane mirrors
Focal length = radius of curvature
Not understanding ray diagrams
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: f = R/2. Mirror equation: 1/f = 1/u + 1/v. Magnification: m = v/u = image height/object height. For concave: f positive. For convex: f negative. Virtual images: v negative.

subtopics.title: Refraction and Lenses
common_misconceptions: Refractive index has units
Critical angle exists for light going from air to water
Power of diverging lens is positive
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Snell's law: n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂. Refractive index n = c/v = sin i/sin r. Critical angle: sin C = 1/n (air to denser). Lens equation: 1/f = 1/u + 1/v. Power P = 1/f (in metres), unit: dioptre (D). Convex: f positive. Concave: f negative.

subtopics.title: Electrostatics
common_misconceptions: Like charges attract
Static electricity is not real electricity
Not understanding grounding
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Coulomb's law: F = kq₁q₂/r² where k = 1/(4πε₀) = 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Electric field: E = F/q = kQ/r². Like charges repel, unlike charges attract. Conservation: total charge in isolated system is constant.

subtopics.title: Current Electricity
common_misconceptions: Current is used up in a circuit
Resistance depends on voltage and current (R = V/I is a ratio, not definition)
Not understanding difference between emf and p.d.
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Ohm's law: V = IR (for ohmic conductors). Resistance: R = ρL/A. Resistivity ρ depends on material. Emf E = I(R + r) where r is internal resistance. Terminal p.d. V = IR = E - Ir. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.

subtopics.title: Electric Circuits
common_misconceptions: Current is the same in parallel branches
Voltage is the same across series components
Adding resistors always increases total resistance
subtopics.teaching_note: Key equations: Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃. For two parallel: R = R₁R₂/(R₁+R₂). Series: same current, voltage divides. Parallel: same voltage, current divides. Kirchhoff's current law: ΣI_in = ΣI_out. Kirchhoff's voltage law: Σemf = ΣIR.
